_meta.authoringNotes: Clinical features and epidemiology per WHO Chikungunya Fact Sheet (2024) and CDC Chikungunya resource (2024). Chronic arthralgia outcomes per Schilte et al. PLoS Negl Trop Dis 2013 (PMID 23556021). Diagnosis approach per Burt et al. Nat Rev Microbiol 2017 (PMID 28804168). IXCHIQ live-attenuated vaccine FDA approval 2023 — first vaccine for chikungunya.

h1Title: Chikungunya: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Chikungunya is a mosquito-borne alphavirus disease whose name translates from the Kimakonde language as 'that which bends up' — a vivid description of the intensely painful joint disease it causes. WHO estimates more than 3 million reported cases across more than 110 countries since the virus re-emerged across the Indian Ocean and the Americas after 2004. Transmission is by Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus mosquitoes biting during the day. After a 3-7 day incubation, patients develop sudden high fever, severe symmetric polyarthritis affecting wrists, ankles, hands, and feet, headache, myalgia, and a maculopapular rash. Mortality is low (under 0.1%) but chronic joint pain persists in 30-40% of patients for months to years. Treatment is supportive; the first chikungunya vaccine (IXCHIQ live-attenuated, FDA-approved November 2023) is now available for adults at risk.

keyStats.prevalence: Over 3 million cases reported across more than 110 countries since 2004 (WHO 2024); India, Brazil, the Caribbean, and the Indian Ocean islands have all experienced major outbreaks
keyStats.demographics: All age groups affected; chronic arthralgia disproportionately affects adults over 40 and women
keyStats.avgAge: Acute disease across all ages; chronic post-chikungunya joint pain peaks in adults over 40
keyStats.globalCases: Endemic transmission ongoing in India, Brazil, Mexico, Caribbean nations, and parts of Africa and Southeast Asia; periodic large outbreaks superimposed

definition: Chikungunya virus disease (ICD-10: A92.0) is an arboviral infection caused by chikungunya virus (CHIKV), a single-stranded RNA alphavirus in the Togaviridae family. The virus is transmitted by Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus mosquitoes; A. albopictus has extended transmission into temperate and Indian Ocean island regions. After a 3-7 day incubation period, infection causes a biphasic illness: an acute febrile arthritic phase lasting 7-14 days, followed in 30-40% of patients by a post-acute or chronic phase of persistent polyarthralgia and arthritis lasting months to years. Acute symptoms include high fever (typically over 39°C), severe symmetric polyarthralgia of small joints, maculopapular rash, headache, myalgia, conjunctival injection, and lymphadenopathy. Mortality is low (under 0.1% of infections) but morbidity is substantial — many patients are unable to work for weeks because of disabling joint pain. Vertical transmission occurs in roughly 50% of pregnancies when maternal viremia overlaps with delivery, causing severe neonatal disease including encephalopathy. Chikungunya frequently co-circulates with dengue and Zika, complicating diagnosis. Management is supportive; the IXCHIQ live-attenuated vaccine was approved by the US FDA in 2023 for adults aged 18 and older at risk of exposure. The condition is managed by infectious disease specialists, tropical medicine physicians, primary care doctors, and rheumatologists when chronic joint involvement persists.

typesClassification.description: First 7-14 days of illness. High fever (often over 39°C), abrupt-onset severe polyarthralgia of wrists, ankles, hands, and feet, maculopapular rash on trunk and limbs, headache, myalgia. Self-limited in most patients.
Persistent joint pain, stiffness, and fatigue continuing after resolution of fever. Affects 30-50% of patients and may overlap with rheumatologic differentials.

Persistent polyarthritis, often symmetrical, affecting the small joints. Sometimes mimics rheumatoid arthritis. Persists in 25-40% of patients at 1 year and 10-20% at 2 years. Mechanism likely involves viral persistence in synovial tissue and immune dysregulation.

Severe disease with encephalopathy, myocarditis, hepatitis, acute respiratory distress, or hemorrhage. Concentrated in neonates, older adults, and patients with comorbidities. Mortality higher in this group.
Vertical transmission near delivery (intrapartum) causes severe neonatal disease including encephalopathy, seizures, hemodynamic instability, and long-term neurodevelopmental impairment.

primarySymptoms: Sudden onset of high fever (often above 39°C) lasting 3-7 days after a 3-7 day incubation following mosquito bite.
Severe symmetric polyarthralgia of the small joints of the hands, wrists, ankles, and feet — the most distinctive feature.
Joint swelling and tenderness, often disabling — patients cannot grip, walk, or perform daily tasks.
Maculopapular rash on the trunk and limbs in 40-75% of patients, appearing 2-5 days after fever onset.
Severe headache, often retro-orbital, lasting several days.
Marked myalgia and back pain accompanying the polyarthralgia.
Fatigue and malaise that can persist long after fever resolves.
Conjunctival injection, photophobia, or eye pain in some patients.
Nausea, vomiting, and abdominal discomfort, especially in pediatric patients.
Lymphadenopathy and bilateral symmetric joint swelling on examination.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden high fever and severe joint pain 3-7 days after travel to or residence in an Aedes mosquito-endemic region
Symmetric joint swelling of wrists, hands, ankles, or feet in a febrile patient with recent travel
Maculopapular rash appearing within days of fever onset
Cluster of similar symptomatic cases in family or community during local outbreak alerts
Severe joint pain combined with conjunctivitis and rash — strongly suggestive triad
emergencySigns: Altered mental status, seizures, focal neurological deficits, or neck stiffness — possible encephalopathy or encephalitis
Chest pain, dyspnea, or new arrhythmia — exclude myocarditis or pericarditis
Severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, or hypotension — exclude concurrent dengue or hepatitis
Neonate with fever, lethargy, seizures, or feeding refusal whose mother had chikungunya around the time of delivery — severe neonatal disease
Acute jaundice, dark urine, or rapidly worsening fatigue — exclude severe hepatitis

causes.cause: Bite of an infected Aedes aegypti or Aedes albopictus mosquito
causes.description: Day-biting Aedes mosquitoes acquire chikungunya virus by feeding on viremic humans and transmit it to others 2-7 days later. A. aegypti is the principal vector in tropical urban areas; A. albopictus extends transmission into temperate regions including parts of Europe and the Indian Ocean.

causes.cause: Outbreak conditions and high vector density
causes.description: Chikungunya outbreaks accompany rising Aedes density (rainy seasons, accumulation of water in containers, urban crowding). The Reunion Island outbreak (2005-2006) infected approximately one-third of the population in a single season.

Pregnant women with viremia at the time of delivery transmit chikungunya to the neonate in roughly 50% of cases. Severe neonatal disease (encephalopathy, seizures, long-term sequelae) is a recognized risk.

Returning travelers with viremia have introduced local outbreaks in southern Europe (Italy 2007, France 2010), the Caribbean (2013), and the continental US (2014). Local vector competence in receiving regions determines whether transmission spreads.

causes.cause: Inadequate vector control around the home and workplace
causes.description: Standing water in flowerpots, tires, water-storage containers, blocked gutters, and discarded bottles all support Aedes breeding. Eliminating these sources cuts local mosquito density and outbreak intensity dramatically.

riskFactors.factor: Residence or travel in an active transmission area
riskFactors.description: Tropical and subtropical regions of the Americas, Caribbean, Africa, South and Southeast Asia, and Indian Ocean islands have ongoing transmission. WHO and CDC maintain country-by-country advisories.

riskFactors.factor: Outdoor activity during mosquito-active daylight hours
riskFactors.description: Aedes mosquitoes bite primarily during the day with peaks at dawn and dusk. Outdoor work, sports, and travel during these hours raise exposure substantially.

Older adults and patients with diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or immunosuppression face higher risk of severe disease and chronic post-chikungunya arthritis. Mortality is concentrated in this group.
Women are at higher risk of chronic post-chikungunya arthritis lasting more than 1 year, possibly because of immune and hormonal factors that also influence rheumatoid arthritis.
Maternal viremia within a few days of delivery transmits to neonate in roughly 50% of cases. Severe neonatal chikungunya is associated with poor neurodevelopmental outcomes.
Standing water in flowerpots, tires, and containers maintains Aedes populations. Eliminating these sources cuts household exposure substantially.
Rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, and lupus patients may experience disease flares triggered by chikungunya infection and have higher rates of prolonged joint symptoms.

affectedDemographics: Reunion Island 2005-2006 outbreak: approximately 266,000 cases in a population of 770,000.
Americas 2013-2015: more than 1.7 million suspected cases reported following introduction to the Caribbean.
India: large recurring outbreaks since 2006, with hundreds of thousands of cases per year in peak years.
Brazil 2014-2017: hundreds of thousands of cases concentrated in the northeast and southeast.
Travel-associated cases reported in over 60 non-endemic countries with periodic local outbreaks in southern Europe and southern US.
diagnosisOverview: Suspect chikungunya in any patient with sudden high fever and severe symmetric polyarthralgia within 7 days of travel to or residence in an active transmission area. Differential diagnosis with dengue and Zika is mandatory in co-endemic regions, and concurrent testing is standard. Diagnosis combines clinical features with molecular and serologic confirmation. Reverse-transcription PCR (RT-PCR) on serum within the first 7-8 days of illness detects viral RNA with high sensitivity; viremia peaks at day 2-4. IgM antibody by ELISA becomes positive 5-7 days after symptom onset and persists for several months; IgG appears later and persists for years. Concurrent dengue NS1 antigen and IgM testing is mandatory because the two viruses share clinical features but have different management implications — most importantly, NSAIDs should be avoided until dengue is excluded because they can worsen hemorrhagic complications. Acute joint examination documents symmetric arthritis of small joints; in patients with persistent symptoms past 4-6 weeks, screening for inflammatory arthritis differentials (rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP, ANA) helps distinguish post-chikungunya arthritis from new-onset rheumatologic disease. Imaging is rarely needed acutely; persistent joint pain past 3 months may warrant ultrasound or MRI for synovitis assessment and rheumatology referral.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects chikungunya viral RNA during acute viremia; most specific test within 7-8 days of symptom onset
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results typically return in 3-7 days from reference laboratories. Most sensitive in the first 5-7 days when viremia is highest.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects antibody response from approximately day 5-7 of illness; persists for several months
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Result usually within 3-5 days. Positive IgM with appropriate clinical features supports diagnosis; concurrent dengue testing is needed for differential.

diagnosticTests.test: Chikungunya IgG ELISA or plaque-reduction neutralization
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms past infection and supports diagnosis when paired with acute IgM; persists for years
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw 2-4 weeks after acute illness. PRNT is more specific and used in research and reference laboratories.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes the two main differential diagnoses, which co-circulate and have overlapping clinical features
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Done from the same blood draw used for chikungunya testing. Dengue NS1 antigen and IgM, plus Zika RT-PCR or IgM as appropriate. Results in 1-7 days depending on platform.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count, liver enzymes, creatinine
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies thrombocytopenia, lymphopenia, hepatitis, and renal involvement; supports differential diagnosis with dengue
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood panel; results within hours. Lymphopenia (under 1,000/µL) is common in acute chikungunya; thrombocytopenia is mild compared with dengue (chikungunya rarely drops below 100,000/µL).

diagnosticTests.test: Rheumatologic workup in chronic cases (RF, anti-CCP, ANA, ESR, CRP)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes post-chikungunya arthritis from new-onset rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, or other inflammatory diseases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 5-10 days. Performed in patients with joint symptoms persisting beyond 3 months.

treatmentOverview: No specific antiviral therapy exists for chikungunya. Management is supportive in the acute phase and tailored to the chronic phase. Acute care includes paracetamol (acetaminophen) for fever and pain, oral fluids, and rest. NSAIDs and aspirin are avoided in the first 48-72 hours and until dengue has been excluded because they can worsen hemorrhagic complications in dengue. After exclusion of dengue, NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily, naproxen 500 mg twice daily) are the mainstay for joint pain. Local cold packs and gentle range-of-motion exercises reduce joint stiffness. Hospitalization is rarely needed for typical acute chikungunya but may be required for severe atypical disease, dehydration, or comorbid conditions. The post-acute and chronic phases are managed with continued NSAIDs, physical therapy, hydroxychloroquine (200-400 mg/day), short courses of low-dose corticosteroids in severely symptomatic cases, and methotrexate or other DMARDs in patients meeting criteria for chronic inflammatory arthritis under rheumatology guidance. Vector control around the home and bite avoidance for the first week of illness prevent onward transmission. The IXCHIQ live-attenuated vaccine, FDA-approved in November 2023 for adults aged 18 and older at increased risk of chikungunya exposure, is administered as a single subcutaneous dose; seroconversion exceeds 95% within 28 days, though long-term durability is still under post-marketing surveillance. Pregnant women should consult an infectious disease specialist regarding vaccine timing because the live-attenuated formulation is contraindicated in pregnancy. Public health measures dominate prevention — vector control, surveillance, traveler advisories, and emergency response to outbreaks.

medicalTreatments.name: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) 500-1000 mg every 4-6 hours (max 4 g/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line analgesic and antipyretic for acute chikungunya. Used in the first 48-72 hours and whenever dengue cannot be excluded. Safer than NSAIDs in the early acute phase.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces fever and pain in over 80% of patients in the acute phase

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily, naproxen 500 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Mainstay of joint pain management after dengue is excluded. Continued for 1-4 weeks in the acute and post-acute phase. Adjust dose for renal function and comorbidities.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction in 60-75% of patients with chikungunya arthralgia

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine 200-400 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Used in patients with persistent inflammatory joint symptoms beyond 6 weeks. Mechanism overlaps with use in rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. Requires baseline ophthalmology assessment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in 40-60% of chronic chikungunya arthritis patients in observational series

medicalTreatments.name: Short-course oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 15-30 mg/day tapered over 4-8 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severely symptomatic patients with significant functional limitation despite NSAIDs and hydroxychloroquine. Used cautiously because of relapse on tapering.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid symptomatic relief in 70-80%; relapse common on rapid taper

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate or other DMARDs (rheumatology-supervised)
medicalTreatments.description: Considered in patients meeting criteria for persistent inflammatory polyarthritis past 3-6 months. Doses and monitoring mirror those used in rheumatoid arthritis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to rheumatoid arthritis cohorts in observational studies of post-chikungunya chronic arthritis

medicalTreatments.name: IXCHIQ chikungunya vaccine (live-attenuated, single subcutaneous dose)
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved in November 2023 for adults 18 years and older at increased risk of chikungunya virus exposure. Contraindicated in pregnancy and severe immunosuppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Seroconversion in over 95% of recipients within 28 days; long-term protection under post-marketing surveillance

surgicalOptions.name: Joint replacement surgery for end-stage post-chikungunya arthritis
surgicalOptions.description: Rare; reserved for selected patients with severe joint destruction unresponsive to long-term medical management. Typically affects knee or hip joints already predisposed to osteoarthritis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Outcomes comparable to joint replacement for other arthritis causes when patient selection is appropriate

alternativeTreatments.name: Physical and occupational therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Graded range-of-motion exercises, strengthening, and joint protection education in the post-acute phase improve function and reduce stiffness. Particularly valuable for hand and wrist involvement.

alternativeTreatments.name: Aquatic exercise programs
alternativeTreatments.description: Warm-water exercise reduces joint loading while maintaining range of motion and aerobic capacity in patients with chronic chikungunya arthritis. Well-tolerated and effective for return to activity.

whySeeSpecialist: Infectious disease referral is recommended for any patient with severe acute chikungunya, atypical presentation, or persistent symptoms beyond 4-6 weeks. Rheumatology involvement is essential for chronic post-chikungunya arthritis lasting more than 3 months. Maternal-fetal medicine evaluates pregnant women diagnosed in the third trimester because of perinatal transmission risk.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an infectious disease specialist or tropical medicine physician with experience in arboviral disease; many academic centers run dedicated travel medicine clinics. Rheumatologists managing post-viral arthritis are best for chronic joint involvement. Verify that the clinic offers chikungunya PCR and serology, plus dengue and Zika testing.
hospitalCriteria: Access to chikungunya RT-PCR and serology, or contracted reference laboratory
Concurrent dengue and Zika testing capability
Rheumatology service for chronic arthritis follow-up
Maternal-fetal medicine clinic for pregnant patients
Vector control coordination with public health authorities
keyFacilities: Rheumatology clinics with post-viral arthritis experience
Public health surveillance and vector control programs
preventionStrategies: Use insect repellent containing DEET (20-30%), picaridin, IR3535, or oil of lemon eucalyptus on exposed skin during the day in endemic regions and reapply per label.
Wear long sleeves, long trousers, and permethrin-treated clothing during peak Aedes activity (dawn and dusk).
Stay in air-conditioned or screened accommodation; sleep under a permethrin-treated bed net if the room is not screened.
Eliminate breeding sites around the home: empty flowerpots, change water in bird baths twice weekly, cover or discard tires and containers, clear blocked gutters.
Consider the IXCHIQ chikungunya vaccine if you are 18 or older and traveling to or working in an active transmission area; consult a travel medicine provider for individual risk assessment.
Use mosquito protection during the first week of illness if infected to prevent onward transmission to vectors and family members.
lifestyleModifications: Rest during the acute phase; gradually increase activity as joint pain allows.
Use cold packs on swollen joints and gentle stretches to reduce stiffness.
Track joint symptoms and functional limitation to share with rheumatologist if persistent past 6 weeks.
Continue insect bite avoidance after symptoms resolve to prevent re-infection (though immunity is typically lifelong, co-infection with dengue or Zika remains possible).
Maintain a healthy body weight and physical conditioning to reduce joint load.
Disclose recent travel and chikungunya history to any clinician evaluating new joint symptoms in the months after acute infection.

recommended: Adequate oral fluids (2-3 liters per day) during the acute febrile illness
Easily digestible foods (rice, soft fruits, broth) during the febrile phase
Anti-inflammatory whole foods (oily fish, vegetables, berries, olive oil) during convalescence
Adequate protein (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to preserve muscle mass during prolonged illness
avoid: Alcohol during the acute phase — interacts with hepatic clearance and rehydration
Aspirin and NSAIDs until dengue is excluded — they can worsen hemorrhagic complications
Crash diets or excessive caloric restriction during recovery from prolonged illness
Unproven herbal 'remedies' marketed for chikungunya — none have proven antiviral effect

exerciseGuidelines: Rest in the acute febrile phase. Once fever resolves and pain is manageable, gradual range-of-motion exercises help prevent stiffness. Pool walking and stationary cycling are often the best-tolerated reintroduction activities. Patients with persistent arthritis benefit from structured physiotherapy with progressive strengthening over 6-12 weeks.
dailyManagement: Take paracetamol for fever and pain; switch to NSAIDs after the first 48-72 hours when dengue has been excluded.
Drink at least 2-3 liters of fluid each day during the acute illness.
Use cold packs on painful swollen joints 10-15 minutes several times a day.
Perform gentle range-of-motion exercises through the day to maintain mobility.
Use mosquito repellent and stay in screened or air-conditioned accommodation during the first week to prevent onward transmission.
Report any new neurological symptoms, chest pain, jaundice, or persistent vomiting urgently.
prognosis: Acute mortality is low (under 0.1% of infections in most outbreaks). Most adults recover from acute disease within 7-14 days. However, post-chikungunya arthralgia persists in 30-40% of patients at 3 months, 25-40% at 1 year, and 10-20% at 2 years. Predictors of chronic joint disease include age over 40, female sex, severity of acute joint symptoms, and pre-existing osteoarthritis or inflammatory arthritis. Quality of life can be substantially affected during the chronic phase. Severe disease in neonates infected perinatally carries higher mortality and rates of long-term neurodevelopmental impairment. Lifelong immunity follows infection in immunocompetent adults; clinically apparent re-infection is rare. The introduction of the IXCHIQ vaccine in 2023 has the potential to change this prognosis in vaccinated populations once experience and supply expand.
recoveryTimeline: Acute symptoms resolve over 7-14 days in most patients. Post-acute joint pain may persist for several weeks. Chronic post-chikungunya arthritis lasts 3 months to several years in 25-40% of patients. Rehabilitation gains continue for 6-12 months in those with chronic disease.
complications: Chronic post-chikungunya arthritis lasting months to years in 25-40% of patients; significant functional impairment in subsets.
Severe atypical disease with encephalopathy, myocarditis, hepatitis, or hemorrhage — concentrated in older adults, neonates, and those with comorbidities; higher mortality.
Severe perinatal infection in neonates of mothers with viremia near delivery, with seizures, encephalopathy, and long-term neurodevelopmental impairment.
Concurrent dengue or Zika infection adding hemorrhagic risk or pregnancy implications.
Acute exacerbation of pre-existing inflammatory or autoimmune arthritis.
Severe depression, anxiety, and disability from prolonged chronic arthritis affecting work and family life.

supportResources.name: WHO Chikungunya
supportResources.description: Global statistics, prevention guidance, vaccine information, and outbreak surveillance for chikungunya.

supportResources.name: CDC Chikungunya Virus
supportResources.description: US public health guidance on diagnosis, prevention, traveler advisories, vaccine recommendations, and laboratory testing.

supportResources.name: Pan American Health Organization (PAHO) Chikungunya
supportResources.description: Regional public health information for the Americas, where major chikungunya outbreaks have occurred.

supportResources.name: International Society for Infectious Diseases (ISID) ProMED-mail
supportResources.description: Real-time global outbreak reporting and surveillance system tracking chikungunya and other arboviral diseases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dengue Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dengue and chikungunya share Aedes vector and overlapping clinical features. Dengue has more pronounced headache, retro-orbital pain, severe thrombocytopenia, and the risk of dengue hemorrhagic fever; chikungunya has more severe and prolonged joint pain. Both must be tested in any compatible patient.

confusedWithConditions.name: Zika Virus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Zika is generally milder with low-grade fever, prominent conjunctivitis, and rash but minimal joint pain. The major Zika risks are congenital infection and Guillain-Barre syndrome rather than chronic arthritis. Both can be tested simultaneously when symptoms overlap.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rheumatoid arthritis shares symmetric small-joint involvement with chronic chikungunya but lacks a single acute febrile onset. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP are typically positive in rheumatoid arthritis; serology and travel history support chikungunya. Both warrant rheumatology evaluation if persistent.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leptospirosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leptospirosis causes fever, myalgia, and conjunctival suffusion in travelers but is bacterial, transmitted through contaminated water, and may cause jaundice and renal failure (Weil's disease). It responds to doxycycline or penicillin; chikungunya is viral and supportive only.

relatedConditions.name: Dengue Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Same Aedes mosquito vector and overlapping clinical features; concurrent testing essential and co-infection possible.

relatedConditions.name: Zika Virus
relatedConditions.relevance: Same vector and overlapping rash and fever; differential diagnosis required in endemic regions.

relatedConditions.name: Yellow Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Another mosquito-borne febrile illness in overlapping tropical regions; vaccination is available and required for many endemic destinations.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Main differential and reference framework for chronic post-chikungunya arthritis management.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Another mosquito-borne febrile illness frequently considered in returning travelers with fever; parasitologically distinct but co-endemic.

faqs.question: What is chikungunya?
faqs.answer: Chikungunya is a viral disease transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes that causes sudden high fever, severe symmetric joint pain, rash, headache, and fatigue. The name means 'that which bends up' in Kimakonde, describing how patients fold over from joint pain. Most recover within two weeks but 30-40% have prolonged joint pain.

faqs.question: How is chikungunya transmitted?
faqs.answer: Chikungunya is transmitted through the bite of infected Aedes aegypti or Aedes albopictus mosquitoes that bite during the day. Vertical transmission from mother to neonate around delivery occurs in approximately half of pregnancies with maternal viremia at term. Person-to-person transmission has not been documented.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of chikungunya?
faqs.answer: Sudden high fever (often over 39°C), severe symmetric joint pain in wrists, ankles, hands, and feet, maculopapular rash, headache, and fatigue are typical. Joint pain is the hallmark — patients often cannot grip or walk. Symptoms appear 3-7 days after a mosquito bite and last 7-14 days.

faqs.question: How long does chikungunya last?
faqs.answer: Acute fever and rash resolve within 7-14 days in most patients. Joint pain may continue for weeks to years; 30-40% of patients have persistent arthralgia at 3 months and 10-20% at 2 years. Predictors of chronic disease include age over 40, female sex, and severity of acute joint symptoms.

faqs.question: Is there a chikungunya vaccine?
faqs.answer: Yes. The IXCHIQ live-attenuated chikungunya vaccine was approved by the US FDA in November 2023 as a single subcutaneous dose for adults 18 and older at increased risk of exposure. Seroconversion exceeds 95% within 28 days. It is contraindicated in pregnancy and severe immunosuppression.

faqs.question: How is chikungunya diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses RT-PCR on serum within the first 7-8 days of illness and IgM serology from day 5-7 onward. Concurrent dengue and Zika testing is essential because all three viruses co-circulate and have overlapping symptoms. Travel history is critical for clinical suspicion.

faqs.question: How is chikungunya treated?
faqs.answer: There is no specific antiviral therapy. Acute care includes paracetamol for fever and pain, oral fluids, and rest. NSAIDs are added after dengue is excluded. Chronic joint pain may need hydroxychloroquine, short-course steroids, methotrexate, or DMARDs under rheumatology guidance.

faqs.question: Can chikungunya kill you?
faqs.answer: Mortality is low (under 0.1% of infections) and concentrated in older adults, neonates infected perinatally, and patients with comorbidities. Most deaths reflect severe atypical disease (encephalopathy, myocarditis, hepatitis) or severe neonatal infection. The high burden is from prolonged disability rather than death.

faqs.question: Can you get chikungunya twice?
faqs.answer: Clinical re-infection is rare. Most immunocompetent adults develop lifelong immunity after a single infection. Persistent or recurrent joint symptoms after acute illness reflect chronic post-chikungunya arthritis rather than re-infection in most cases.

faqs.question: How do you prevent chikungunya?
faqs.answer: Use DEET, picaridin, or IR3535 insect repellent during the day, wear long sleeves and trousers, stay in air-conditioned or screened accommodation, and eliminate standing water around the home. The IXCHIQ vaccine is now available for adults at increased risk. Travelers should follow CDC and WHO travel advisories.

faqs.question: Is chikungunya the same as dengue?
faqs.answer: No. Both are transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes and share fever and rash, but chikungunya causes more severe and prolonged joint pain while dengue causes more headache, retro-orbital pain, severe thrombocytopenia, and risk of hemorrhagic complications. Both should be tested in any compatible patient.

faqs.question: Can pregnant women get chikungunya?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pregnant women infected late in pregnancy can transmit the virus to the newborn at delivery in approximately 50% of cases, causing severe neonatal disease with encephalopathy and long-term neurodevelopmental sequelae. Pregnant travelers should follow strict mosquito-bite prevention; the IXCHIQ vaccine is contraindicated in pregnancy.

faqs.question: Why does chikungunya cause such severe joint pain?
faqs.answer: Chikungunya virus infects synovial cells and triggers persistent local inflammation in joint tissue. Viral RNA has been detected in synovial samples months after acute infection, and immune dysregulation contributes to chronic arthritis. The same mechanisms underlie acute fever, rash, and chronic post-viral arthropathy.

faqs.question: Can children get chikungunya?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children develop similar acute symptoms with high fever, rash, and joint pain. Severity is generally lower than in adults, but neonates infected perinatally can develop severe disease with encephalopathy and long-term neurodevelopmental impairment. Pediatric chikungunya is managed supportively with paracetamol and fluids.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for chikungunya joint pain?
faqs.answer: Paracetamol in the first 48-72 hours; NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) once dengue is excluded. For pain persisting beyond 6 weeks, hydroxychloroquine, short-course corticosteroids, or methotrexate may be added under specialist supervision. Physical therapy and graded exercise help maintain joint function.

faqs.question: Can chikungunya cause permanent disability?
faqs.answer: Persistent joint pain and stiffness lasting years occur in 10-20% of patients at 2 years; severe functional impairment is less common but documented. End-stage joint destruction requiring surgery is rare. Early structured physical therapy and rheumatology care reduce long-term disability.

faqs.question: Should I cancel travel during a chikungunya outbreak?
faqs.answer: Pregnant women and people with significant comorbidities should weigh travel carefully and consult a travel medicine provider. Other travelers should follow strict mosquito-bite prevention. The IXCHIQ vaccine may be considered for adults visiting active transmission areas. Check current advisories from CDC and WHO before booking.

faqs.question: Can chikungunya be transmitted by blood transfusion?
faqs.answer: Theoretically yes, because viremia is high in the first week of illness. Blood services in outbreak areas may defer donors who report recent fever or travel. Documented transfusion transmission is rare but possible, and screening or deferral policies vary by country.

faqs.question: Does chikungunya cause hair loss?
faqs.answer: Yes, in some patients. Telogen effluvium (diffuse hair shedding 2-4 months after a febrile illness) is reported after chikungunya as it is after other significant infections. Hair regrows over 6-12 months without specific treatment.

faqs.question: When should I see a rheumatologist after chikungunya?
faqs.answer: See a rheumatologist if joint pain, swelling, or stiffness persists beyond 6-12 weeks after the acute illness, especially if it affects function or sleep. Rheumatologists distinguish post-chikungunya arthritis from new-onset inflammatory arthritis and guide use of hydroxychloroquine, methotrexate, or other DMARDs.

faqs.question: Is there a cure for chikungunya?
faqs.answer: No specific antiviral cure exists. Treatment is supportive, and most patients recover spontaneously. Chronic post-chikungunya arthritis is managed with NSAIDs, hydroxychloroquine, and other rheumatologic agents under specialist care. The IXCHIQ vaccine prevents future infection but does not treat existing disease.

metaDescription: Chikungunya: mosquito-borne illness with severe joint pain and fever. Symptoms, diagnosis, treatment, IXCHIQ vaccine and prevention, medically reviewed.
